specification.topic-statement: The provider HTTP lifecycle system manages the asynchronous HTTP request pipeline for streaming and non-streaming LLM API calls across all three providers, including socket readiness queries, transfer progress, timeout retrieval, completion detection, and request cancellation.

scope.in-scope: The vtable operations common to all three providers — socket file-descriptor set population, transfer progress driving, timeout retrieval, completed-transfer processing, streaming request initiation, non-streaming request initiation, and cancellation. Provider construction, including base URL resolution and API key handling. The distinction between providers that handle completion locally versus those that delegate entirely to the HTTP multi layer.
scope.boundary: The underlying multi-handle that manages actual socket I/O, transfer state, and completion callbacks is an opaque subsystem. This specification covers only how providers interact with it through its public interface, not how it operates internally.

Raw bytes arriving over the wire are fed into an SSE parser. The parser's framing and event-extraction logic is out of scope; this specification covers only that the write callback feeds data to the parser and processes the resulting events.

The construction of request JSON bodies and URL paths is performed by provider-specific serialization modules. This specification records that serialization occurs as a step of streaming and non-streaming initiation, not how the body is constructed.
Assembling a final LLM response from accumulated streaming state is delegated to per-provider response modules and is out of scope here.
Provider-specific stream event handlers that interpret SSE event types and data payloads into typed stream events are out of scope.

field.name: API key
field.note: copied into provider context; OpenAI additionally validates non-empty at construction time and returns an error if empty; Anthropic and Google do not validate emptiness at construction

field.name: base URL
field.note: read from an environment variable at construction time; falls back to a hardcoded default if the variable is absent; Anthropic default is https://api.anthropic.com; OpenAI default is the hardcoded OpenAI endpoint constant; Google default is https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta

field.name: use_responses_api flag (OpenAI only)
field.note: when true, forces use of the Responses API regardless of model; OpenAI exposes a simple constructor that sets this to false and an extended constructor that accepts it explicitly

field.name: SSE parser
field.note: created at stream start; receives raw bytes from the write callback

field.name: stream context
field.note: created at stream start; holds per-provider accumulated streaming state passed to event handlers

field.name: completion callback
field.note: invoked exactly once when the transfer completes; carries success flag, HTTP status, response, error category, and error message

field.name: completion flag
field.note: set to true by the HTTP-level completion callback when the transfer ends; read by info_read to trigger response building and callback invocation

field.name: HTTP status code
field.note: recorded by the HTTP-level completion callback; used in info_read to determine success and map error category

field.name: http_status
field.note: raw HTTP status code from the completed transfer

field.name: response
field.note: non-null only on success; built from accumulated streaming context; null when stream context is absent

field.name: error_category
field.note: AUTH for 401/403; RATE_LIMIT for 429; SERVER for 500+; UNKNOWN for all other non-success statuses; always UNKNOWN on success

field.name: error_message
field.note: formatted as 'HTTP N' where N is the status code; null on success; allocated transiently and freed immediately after the callback returns

field.name: retry_after_ms
field.note: always -1 at this layer; retry-after header parsing is not performed here

behavior.provider-construction:
Each provider is instantiated by supplying a talloc parent context and an API key. Construction allocates a provider structure and an implementation context as a talloc child of that structure, copies the API key, resolves the base URL from the environment with a hardcoded fallback, and creates an HTTP multi-handle as a child of the implementation context.
OpenAI construction validates that the API key is non-empty before allocating anything; an empty key causes an immediate error return. OpenAI also exposes a second constructor variant that accepts a boolean flag selecting between the Chat API and the Responses API.
Anthropic and Google do not validate key emptiness at construction time.
Google passes the API key in the request URL rather than in a header; this is reflected in request construction but not in the provider context layout.
All three providers set the active stream slot to null at construction time. The active stream slot holds at most one in-flight streaming context at any moment.
behavior.fdset: The fdset operation populates read, write, and exception file-descriptor sets from the HTTP multi-handle and updates the caller's maximum file-descriptor value. All three providers implement this identically by delegating unconditionally to the multi-handle.
behavior.perform: The perform operation drives the HTTP multi-handle to make progress on all active transfers, updating the count of still-running handles. All three providers implement this identically by delegating unconditionally to the multi-handle.
behavior.timeout: The timeout operation retrieves the multi-handle's recommended select timeout in milliseconds. All three providers implement this identically by delegating unconditionally to the multi-handle.
behavior.info-read:
The info_read operation polls for completed transfers and processes any that have finished. Providers differ in whether they handle completion locally or delegate entirely to the multi-handle.
Anthropic calls the multi-handle's info_read through a mockable internal wrapper, then separately inspects the active stream slot. If the active stream exists and its completion flag is set, Anthropic performs local completion handling:
1. A completion record is assembled. If the HTTP status is 200–299, success is true and the response is built from the accumulated streaming context (or null if no streaming context exists). If the status is outside 200–299, success is false, the response is null, the error category is mapped from the status code (401/403 → AUTH; 429 → RATE_LIMIT; 500+ → SERVER; else → UNKNOWN), and an error message is formatted as "HTTP N".
2. The completion callback is invoked with the assembled record.
3. The error message string, if allocated, is freed immediately after the callback returns.
4. The active stream is freed and the active stream slot is set to null.
Google follows an identical pattern to Anthropic: it calls the multi-handle's info_read directly (not through the mockable wrapper), then inspects the active stream slot, performs the same HTTP-status-to-error-category mapping, invokes the completion callback, frees the error message if present, frees the active stream, and clears the active stream slot.
OpenAI delegates entirely to the multi-handle's info_read and performs no local completion handling. There is no active stream slot and no post-call inspection. Completion callbacks are registered directly with the multi-handle at request submission time and are invoked by the multi-handle itself when transfers complete.
Because OpenAI registers its completion handlers with the multi-handle at request submission time, the multi-handle dispatches them directly without any provider-level interception. This differs from Anthropic and Google, which use a two-phase approach: the HTTP-level callback records the status and sets a completion flag, and the provider-level info_read then builds the response and invokes the application-level callback.
Both Anthropic and Google apply the same mapping: 401 or 403 → AUTH; 429 → RATE_LIMIT; 500 or above → SERVER; any other non-success status → UNKNOWN. This mapping is not applied for OpenAI at this layer.
behavior.write-callback:
When streaming is active, the HTTP layer delivers incoming bytes to a write callback registered at stream start. The callback feeds the raw bytes into the SSE parser, then drains all complete events from the parser in a loop. For each event, the event type and data fields are extracted (defaulting to empty strings if absent) and passed to the provider's stream event processor. The event is freed after processing. The callback always reports that it consumed all bytes, even if the stream context or parser is absent.
Google does not use the event type field; only the data field is passed to its stream event processor.
The HTTP-level completion callback is separate from the write callback. It records the HTTP status code and sets the completion flag on the active stream when the transfer ends.
behavior.start-stream:
Initiating a streaming request follows this sequence:
1. An active stream context is allocated as a talloc child of the provider implementation context.
2. A provider-specific streaming context (holding accumulated event state) is created as a child of the active stream.
3. An SSE parser is created as a child of the active stream.
4. The completion callback, completion context, and HTTP status/completion fields are initialized on the active stream.
5. The request URL is constructed from the base URL. For Google, the model name and API key are embedded as URL parameters. For Anthropic, the path is `/v1/messages`. For OpenAI, the path depends on the API mode (Chat or Responses) as determined by a per-model routing check.
6. The request body JSON is serialized with streaming enabled.
7. Request headers are assembled. Anthropic includes `Content-Type: application/json`, `anthropic-version: 2023-06-01`, an `x-api-key` header carrying the API key, and `Accept: text/event-stream`. Google includes only `Content-Type: application/json` and `Accept: text/event-stream` (no authorization header; the API key is in the URL). OpenAI headers are assembled by a shared header builder.
8. The active stream is stored in the provider context.
9. The HTTP request is submitted to the multi-handle with the write callback and HTTP-level completion callback both pointing to the active stream context.
If URL construction or body serialization fails, the active stream is freed and the slot is cleared before returning the error.
The active stream is created during stream initiation and freed during info_read after the completion callback returns. It is not freed in the write callback or completion callback themselves.
behavior.start-request:
Non-streaming request initiation follows the same URL construction, body serialization, and header assembly steps as streaming initiation, but no write callback is registered with the multi-handle. The response body is received atomically rather than incrementally.
For Anthropic and Google, non-streaming initiation is fully delegated to provider-specific request modules. For OpenAI, the request context is created inline, the API mode is selected, and the request is submitted with a completion handler that is called by the multi-handle upon transfer completion.
Both streaming and non-streaming OpenAI requests register their completion handlers directly with the multi-handle. The provider-level info_read is a pure delegation in both cases.
behavior.cancel:
Cancellation removes all active requests from the multi-handle. For Anthropic and Google, cancellation additionally sets the active stream's completion flag to true if an active stream exists. This causes the next info_read call to process the stream as completed and invoke the completion callback with whatever partial state was accumulated.
For OpenAI, cancellation only removes requests from the multi-handle. There is no active stream slot and no additional flag to set.
After cancellation, the active stream for Anthropic and Google is not freed immediately. It persists until the next info_read call observes the completed flag and performs normal completion processing.
behavior.internal-wrapping:
OpenAI exposes a set of internal wrapper functions for request serialization, URL construction, and header building. In debug builds, these wrappers are thin pass-throughs to the real implementations; in release builds, the real functions are called directly. This dual-build pattern allows tests to substitute mock implementations for the internal wrappers without affecting production behavior.
Anthropic similarly wraps the multi-handle info_read call through a mockable internal seam, distinct from the non-wrapped version used by OpenAI and Google.
